terms.definition: DVSA's rolling 12-month rating of a UK operator's compliance risk, banded GREEN < AMBER < RED. RED rating triggers loss-of-repute risk and likely Public Inquiry.
terms.applies_to: UK heavy goods and PSV operators

terms.definition: Second-generation digital tachograph mandatory on new HGVs from Aug 2023 with GNSS border-crossing detection and DSRC remote roadside enforcement. Retrofit required on EU/UK international vehicles by Dec 2024.
terms.applies_to: HGV and coach operators above 3.5t (2.5t intl from 2026)

terms.definition: First generation of smart tachograph fitted to vehicles registered Jun 2019 to Aug 2023 with GNSS but without DSRC. Being phased out via mandatory retrofit to V2.
terms.applies_to: HGV and coach operators

terms.full_name: EU/AETR Drivers' Hours and Rest Periods
terms.definition: Maximum 9h daily driving (10h twice a week), 56h weekly, 90h fortnightly, with 45-min break after 4.5h and 11h daily rest. Enforced via tachograph records.
terms.applies_to: Drivers of vehicles over 3.5t and PSVs 9+ seats

terms.full_name: European Agreement Concerning the Work of Crews of Vehicles Engaged in International Road Transport
terms.definition: UN-led drivers' hours framework that mirrors EU rules for journeys involving non-EU AETR states (e.g. Russia, Turkey, Ukraine). Applied via tachograph in the same way.
terms.applies_to: International HGV/PSV operators crossing AETR contracting parties

terms.definition: UK transposition of EU Directive 2002/15/EC capping mobile workers at 48h/week average, 60h max in any week, with night-work limit of 10h. Sits on top of drivers' hours rules.
terms.applies_to: Mobile workers in road transport

terms.definition: Licence from a Traffic Commissioner required to operate any vehicle above 3.5t for hire/reward or 2.5t internationally. Demands good repute, financial standing and professional competence.
terms.applies_to: UK goods and passenger transport operators

terms.definition: Independent statutory regulator who grants, varies and revokes operator licences and disciplines drivers. Eight commissioners cover the UK regions.
terms.applies_to: UK goods and PSV operators and LGV/PCV drivers

terms.definition: Formal hearing where a Traffic Commissioner considers whether to revoke, suspend or curtail an operator's licence after serious compliance failure. Can disqualify directors and transport managers.
terms.applies_to: UK operators facing repute, financial or competence concerns

terms.definition: Statutory qualification a nominated transport manager must hold to support an O-licence. Covers law, finance, vehicle standards and operations.
terms.regulator: DVSA / OCR / Chartered Institute of Logistics and Transport
terms.applies_to: Nominated transport managers on UK O-licences

terms.definition: 35 hours of periodic training every 5 years required for professional LGV/PCV drivers. Held alongside the vocational licence.
terms.applies_to: Professional LGV and PCV drivers in the UK and EU

terms.definition: Voluntary DVSA scheme where audited operators share KPI data and get fewer roadside stops in return. Requires accredited IT systems and quarterly KPI returns.
terms.applies_to: Established UK operators with at least 2 years on O-licence

terms.definition: Annual roadworthiness test for private passenger vehicles with 13+ seats (e.g. minibuses, coaches not in O-licence use). Carried out at Authorised Testing Facilities.
terms.applies_to: Private 13+ seat passenger vehicles

terms.definition: Annual test for goods vehicles with design gross weight 3,000–3,500 kg, sitting between car (Class 4) and HGV annual test. Carried out by approved garages.
terms.applies_to: Light goods vehicles up to 3.5t

terms.definition: Defunct executive agency merged in 2014 with VCA functions into DVSA. Older paperwork, ATF inspection plates and case law still reference VOSA.
terms.applies_to: Historic UK road transport enforcement references

terms.definition: Time-based charge on HGVs ≥12t using UK roads, reinstated Aug 2023 with environmental weighting based on Euro emission class. Paid alongside VED for UK-registered HGVs.
terms.applies_to: UK and foreign HGVs ≥12t

terms.definition: Daily emission charge zone (London-wide since Aug 2023) for vehicles failing Euro 4 (petrol) / Euro 6 (diesel) standards. HGV/coach equivalent is the LEZ.
terms.applies_to: Vehicles operating within Greater London

terms.definition: London-wide charging zone for heavy diesel vehicles failing Euro VI emission standards, separate from ULEZ which targets lighter vehicles. Daily charges of up to £300.
terms.applies_to: HGVs, buses and coaches in Greater London

terms.definition: TfL star-rating of HGV cab vision; from Oct 2024 vehicles need 3-star or a Progressive Safe System fitted to operate in Greater London. Two-star permits expired.
terms.applies_to: HGVs >12t entering Greater London

terms.definition: Common brand of HGV blind-spot camera/monitor system used to meet DVS Progressive Safe System and FORS Silver vehicle requirements. Often paired with ultrasonic side-scan sensors.
terms.applies_to: HGV and bus operators meeting DVS/FORS

terms.definition: Camera-based driver assistance system providing forward-collision, pedestrian and lane-departure warnings, widely fitted to UK fleets to meet FORS Silver. Owned by Intel.
terms.applies_to: Bus, coach, HGV and van fleets

terms.definition: Entry tier of FORS confirming an operator meets legal compliance, vehicle safety basics and driver-licence checking. Audited on site.
terms.applies_to: UK fleet operators (mainly construction logistics)

terms.definition: Mid-tier FORS requiring vehicle safety kit (cameras, sensors, side-guards), driver training (Safe Urban Driving) and KPI reporting. Required by many TfL/construction contracts.
terms.applies_to: UK fleet operators on safety-critical contracts

terms.definition: Top FORS tier requiring 12 months at Silver plus measurable annual improvement in fuel, emissions, safety and noise. Recognised mark of best-in-class operator.
terms.applies_to: Top-performing UK fleet operators

terms.full_name: Construction Logistics and Community Safety Standard
terms.definition: Construction-industry national standard for managing vehicle and work-related road risk on projects. Mirrors FORS Silver expectations and is mandated by many Tier-1 contractors.
terms.applies_to: Construction logistics operators and clients

terms.full_name: BS 7121 Code of Practice for Safe Use of Cranes
terms.definition: British Standard suite covering planning, supervision and operation of cranes including lorry loaders (Part 4). Used as benchmark for LOLER compliance.
terms.applies_to: Operators of cranes and lorry loaders

terms.full_name: Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations 1998
terms.definition: UK regulations requiring lifting equipment to be strong, stable, marked, examined every 6/12 months and used under a safe plan of work. Backed by HSE prosecutions.
terms.applies_to: All UK employers using lifting equipment

terms.full_name: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations 1998
terms.definition: Underpinning UK regulations requiring work equipment to be suitable, maintained, inspected and used only by trained people. Applies to vehicles and lifting kit alongside LOLER.
terms.applies_to: All UK employers providing work equipment

terms.full_name: Association of Lorry Loader Manufacturers and Importers
terms.definition: UK trade body certifying lorry-loader operator training and Thorough Examinations of loader cranes. ALLMI cards are accepted by FORS and major construction clients.
terms.applies_to: Lorry-loader (HIAB) operators and trainers

terms.definition: UK trade body that publishes the model conditions used in Hire vs Contract Lift agreements for cranes and lorry loaders. Defines who carries the risk on a lift.
terms.applies_to: Crane hire companies and customers

terms.full_name: CPA Crane Hire vs Contract Lift Conditions
terms.definition: Two CPA contract models: Crane Hire (customer is duty-holder, plans the lift) vs Contract Lift (crane supplier takes responsibility, plans and insures the lift). Choice drives liability.
terms.applies_to: Crane and lorry-loader hire transactions

terms.definition: Competent person who plans every lifting operation, selects equipment and writes the lift plan/method statement. Mandatory role under BS 7121.
terms.applies_to: Crane and lorry-loader operations

terms.definition: Site-based competent person who directly supervises the lift in line with the Appointed Person's plan. CPCS/NPORS qualified.
terms.applies_to: Crane lifting operations

terms.definition: Trained person who attaches loads to the crane hook, selects accessories and signals the crane operator. Often combined with banksman duties.
terms.applies_to: Crane and lorry-loader operations

terms.definition: Trained person who guides vehicles during reversing or manoeuvring in yards and sites. Used to control workplace transport risk under HSWA.
terms.applies_to: Yards, depots and construction sites

terms.full_name: Driver, Workshop, Company and Control Tachograph Cards
terms.definition: Four smartcard types used with digital/smart tachographs: Driver (records activity), Workshop (calibration), Company (download/locks), Control (enforcement). Issued in UK by DVLA.
terms.applies_to: Drivers, fitters, operators, enforcement officers

terms.definition: Driver-entered tachograph activity for time when the card was not in the vehicle (rest, other work, ferry/train). Errors are a common roadside infringement.
terms.applies_to: Drivers using digital or smart tachographs

terms.full_name: Global Navigation Satellite System position recording
terms.definition: Smart tachograph feature that records vehicle position at start/end of work and every 3 hours of accumulated driving, plus at border crossings. Used to evidence cabotage compliance.
terms.applies_to: Vehicles fitted with smart tachograph

terms.definition: 5.8 GHz radio link used by Smart Tachograph 2 to let enforcement officers read a small set of tacho data from the roadside without stopping the vehicle. Targets risky vehicles for stops.
terms.applies_to: Vehicles fitted with Smart Tachograph 2

terms.definition: Roadside receiver that pulls the DSRC tachograph beacon to flag operators with infringements. Operated by DVSA traffic examiners and overseas counterparts.
terms.applies_to: Enforcement of Smart Tachograph 2 vehicles

terms.definition: Certification (UK GB/NI or EU WVTA) that a vehicle, tachograph or other component meets construction and use standards before sale. Required for fleet registration.
terms.applies_to: Vehicle manufacturers, importers and converters

terms.definition: Part of the EU Mobility Package: drivers performing cabotage or non-bilateral cross-trade must be paid host-state wages and registered in IMI. Bilateral and transit work is exempt.
terms.applies_to: Operators sending drivers into EU member states

terms.full_name: EU Cabotage Rule (3 operations in 7 days)
terms.definition: Hauliers may carry out up to 3 cabotage trips inside another EU state within 7 days of an inbound international load, then must observe a 4-day cooling-off before returning. Tracked via GNSS.
terms.applies_to: International hauliers operating inside the EU

terms.definition: EU-wide portal used to lodge driver posting declarations and exchange enforcement information between member states. UK operators must declare each posted driver pre-trip.
terms.applies_to: Operators posting drivers into the EU/EEA

terms.definition: Bundle of EU laws on driver posting, cabotage, vehicle return, smart tachograph and access to the occupation. Phased in 2020–2026.
terms.applies_to: All EU/EEA road transport operators (and UK via TCA)

terms.definition: International customs transit document issued under the IRU TIR Convention that lets sealed goods cross multiple borders without intermediate customs checks. Backed by a guarantee chain.
terms.applies_to: International road hauliers crossing TIR contracting parties

terms.definition: Customs transit declaration used inside the EU/EFTA Common Transit area to move non-Union goods under duty suspension between offices of departure and destination. Replaces TIR within CT.
terms.applies_to: Hauliers and freight forwarders within Common Transit

terms.definition: EU/UK system used to lodge and discharge T1/T2 transit declarations electronically. NCTS Phase 5 went live across CT contracting parties in 2024–25.
terms.applies_to: Customs agents, hauliers, transit principals

terms.full_name: CMR Consignment Note (Convention on the Contract for the International Carriage of Goods by Road)
terms.definition: Standardised international consignment note governing liability and proof of delivery on cross-border road journeys. Required for international moves between contracting states.
terms.applies_to: International road carriers and shippers

terms.definition: Annual airline industry manual that operationalises ICAO Technical Instructions for shipping dangerous goods by air. Mandatory training for shippers, packers and freight forwarders.
terms.applies_to: Air freight shippers, forwarders, ground handlers

terms.full_name: ICAO Technical Instructions for the Safe Transport of Dangerous Goods by Air
terms.definition: International legal baseline for air dangerous-goods carriage; IATA DGR mirrors and extends it. Updated every two years.
terms.applies_to: Air operators and dangerous-goods shippers worldwide

terms.full_name: UN Manual of Tests and Criteria — Section 38.3 (Lithium Batteries)
terms.definition: Test regime that lithium-ion and lithium-metal batteries must pass before transport by road, rail, sea or air. Test summary documents are now required up the supply chain.
terms.regulator: UN Sub-Committee of Experts on Transport of Dangerous Goods
terms.applies_to: Manufacturers and shippers of lithium batteries

terms.full_name: European Agreement on Dangerous Goods by Road / Rail
terms.definition: Mandatory rules for moving dangerous goods by road (ADR) and rail (RID) in Europe, covering classification, packaging, vehicles and driver training. Reviewed biennially.
terms.applies_to: European hauliers and rail freight moving DG

terms.definition: IMO rulebook for shipping dangerous goods by sea, covering classification, packaging, stowage and documentation. Aligns with ADR/RID and the UN Model Regulations.
terms.applies_to: Sea carriers, port operators and shippers

terms.full_name: International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea
terms.definition: IMO master convention on ship safety covering construction, fire protection, life-saving, cargo securing and Verified Gross Mass declarations for containers. Underpins IMDG and ECA rules.
terms.applies_to: Ship operators, port states, shippers of containerised cargo

terms.full_name: International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships — Annex VI
terms.definition: IMO rules on ship air emissions, including sulphur cap (0.50% globally / 0.10% in ECAs) and NOx limits. Underpins Emission Control Areas worldwide.
terms.applies_to: Ocean carriers, ferry operators, bunker suppliers

terms.definition: Sea areas designated under MARPOL Annex VI with stricter SOx, NOx or PM limits, e.g. North Sea, Baltic, North American ECAs and (from 2025) Mediterranean SECA. Drives use of low-sulphur fuel.
terms.applies_to: Ships transiting designated ECAs

terms.full_name: EU Emissions Trading System — Maritime Extension
terms.definition: Since Jan 2024 ships >5,000 GT calling at EU ports must surrender allowances for 50% of voyage CO2 and 100% of intra-EU CO2 emissions. Costs are typically passed to cargo.
terms.applies_to: Ocean carriers calling at EU/EEA ports

terms.definition: Mandates a tightening cap on greenhouse-gas intensity of energy used on board ships calling at EU ports, from -2% in 2025 to -80% by 2050. Pairs with EU ETS Maritime.
terms.applies_to: Ships >5,000 GT calling at EU ports

terms.definition: UN agency that sets binding standards for international civil aviation, including dangerous goods, environmental and security rules. UK CAA and EASA align with ICAO Annexes.
terms.applies_to: States, airlines, air cargo operators

terms.definition: US Department of Transportation agency regulating interstate motor carriers, drivers and CMV safety. Issues DOT Numbers and Operating Authority (MC numbers).
terms.applies_to: Interstate motor carriers, freight brokers, drivers

terms.definition: US drivers' hours rules: 11h driving / 14h on-duty / 70h in 8 days for property carriers, with 30-min break after 8h driving. Tracked via ELD.
terms.applies_to: US interstate CMV drivers

terms.definition: US rule requiring most interstate CMV drivers to record HOS using a self-certified ELD instead of paper logs. Active since Dec 2017 (AOBRD grandfathering ended 2019).
terms.applies_to: US interstate CMV drivers and carriers

terms.definition: FMCSA programme using roadside inspections and crash data to identify higher-risk carriers via the Safety Measurement System. Drives interventions and audits.
terms.applies_to: All US interstate motor carriers

terms.full_name: Behavior Analysis and Safety Improvement Category
terms.definition: Seven CSA categories (Unsafe Driving, HOS, Driver Fitness, Drugs/Alcohol, Vehicle Maintenance, HM Compliance, Crash Indicator) used to rank carriers in the SMS. Five are public.
terms.applies_to: US interstate motor carriers

terms.definition: FMCSA percentile ranking of a carrier in each BASIC, where higher percentile = worse performance versus peers. Triggers warning letters and intervention.
terms.applies_to: US interstate motor carriers

terms.definition: Unique identifier the FMCSA issues to interstate carriers, used to track safety, audits and inspections. Required before operating CMVs across state lines.
terms.applies_to: US interstate motor carriers

terms.full_name: Network Licence (Network Rail) / Railway Operator Licences
terms.definition: ORR-issued licences required to operate trains, stations or the network in Great Britain. Sets safety, performance and access conditions.
terms.applies_to: Train operators, freight operators, Network Rail

terms.definition: Independent UK safety and economic regulator for rail and for monitoring Highways England. Licenses and enforces rail safety under ROGS.
terms.applies_to: Mainline rail, light rail, Highways England oversight

terms.full_name: Railways and Other Guided Transport Systems (Safety) Regulations 2006
terms.definition: Core GB rail safety regulations requiring safety management systems, safety certification of train operators and authorisation of infrastructure managers. Enforced by ORR.
terms.applies_to: Rail and other guided transport operators in GB

terms.definition: Independent body that investigates UK rail accidents and incidents to improve safety, without apportioning blame. Reports inform ORR and operators.
terms.applies_to: UK rail and other guided transport operators

terms.definition: ROGS safety certificate authorising a train operator to use specific routes and rolling stock. Issued by ORR following acceptance of the safety management system.
terms.applies_to: GB train and freight operating companies

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Annex I — Union Harmonisation Legislation
terms.definition: List of EU product-safety laws (e.g. machinery, vehicles, medical devices) where AI safety components are automatically high-risk when subject to third-party conformity assessment. Annex I obligations apply from Aug 2027 (12-month delay confirmed under Digital Omnibus).
terms.applies_to: Manufacturers embedding AI in EU-regulated products

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Annex III — Standalone High-Risk Use Cases
terms.definition: Enumerated standalone high-risk AI use cases (e.g. recruitment, credit, education, critical infrastructure, biometrics, law enforcement). High-risk obligations now apply from Dec 2027 under the Digital Omnibus 16-month delay.
terms.applies_to: Providers and deployers of Annex III AI systems in the EU

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Article 9 — Risk Management System
terms.definition: Requires providers of high-risk AI to run a continuous, documented risk-management process across the system's lifecycle. Outputs feed the technical file and conformity assessment.
terms.applies_to: Providers of EU high-risk AI systems

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Article 10 — Data and Data Governance
terms.definition: Requires training, validation and test data for high-risk AI to be relevant, representative, free of errors and managed for bias. Includes data-governance documentation.
terms.applies_to: Providers of EU high-risk AI systems

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Article 13 — Transparency and Information to Deployers
terms.definition: Requires high-risk AI to be transparent enough for deployers to interpret outputs and to come with clear instructions for use, capabilities and limitations. Underpins downstream deployer obligations.
terms.applies_to: Providers and deployers of EU high-risk AI

terms.full_name: EU AI Act Article 15 — Accuracy, Robustness, Cybersecurity
terms.definition: Requires high-risk AI to declare and maintain accuracy levels, resilience against errors/faults and cybersecurity controls. Levels are documented in the instructions for use.
terms.applies_to: Providers of EU high-risk AI systems

terms.full_name: Automated Decision-Making Safeguards (Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, s.80 — replaces UK GDPR Art. 22)
terms.definition: UK statutory safeguards on solely automated decisions with legal/significant effect: must give the individual information, allow human review and contest the decision. Successor to UK GDPR Article 22.
terms.applies_to: UK controllers using ADM with legal or significant effect

terms.definition: EU rule giving data subjects the right not to be subject to a solely automated decision with legal or significant effect, with exceptions and a right to human intervention. Still in force in the EU.
terms.applies_to: Controllers using ADM in the EU

terms.full_name: UK GDPR Article 22 — Automated Decision-Making (legacy reference)
terms.definition: Pre-DUA-Act position: solely automated decisions with legal effect were generally prohibited without consent or contract. Now restated and broadened via Data (Use and Access) Act 2025.
terms.applies_to: UK controllers (historic, replaced by DUA Act safeguards)

terms.definition: Risk assessment required when processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals, including most large-scale or novel AI processing. Mandatory under UK/EU GDPR Article 35.
terms.applies_to: Controllers processing high-risk personal data

terms.full_name: Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (EU AI Act Article 27)
terms.definition: Pre-deployment assessment required from public-sector and certain private deployers of Annex III high-risk AI, covering affected groups, harms and mitigations. Complements (not replaces) a DPIA.
terms.applies_to: EU public bodies and named private deployers of high-risk AI

terms.definition: Voluntary US framework structured around four functions — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE — for managing risks of AI systems. Widely used as evidence of due diligence.
terms.applies_to: Any organisation designing, deploying or using AI

terms.definition: First certifiable management-system standard for AI, modelled on ISO 27001. Provides controls covering AI policy, roles, risk, lifecycle and supplier management.
terms.regulator: ISO/IEC (standards body); certification by accredited bodies
terms.applies_to: Organisations operating AI systems

terms.full_name: Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI, Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law
terms.definition: First binding international treaty on AI, opened for signature Sep 2024 and signed by the UK, US and EU. Sets obligations around lifecycle risk, transparency and remedies.
terms.applies_to: Signatory states (public sector by default; private sector via national law)

terms.definition: Process by which a high-risk AI system is checked against EU AI Act requirements, then CE-marked. Usually self-assessment, with notified-body assessment for some Annex I products.
terms.applies_to: Providers placing high-risk AI on the EU market

terms.definition: An AI system listed in Annex I or III of the EU AI Act, triggering full lifecycle obligations: risk management, data governance, human oversight, logging, transparency and post-market monitoring.
terms.applies_to: Providers and deployers of qualifying AI in the EU

terms.full_name: General-Purpose AI Model / Foundation Model (EU AI Act Title VIIIa)
terms.definition: Large AI model trained on broad data, usable across many tasks (e.g. GPT, Claude, Llama). EU AI Act imposes provider duties from Aug 2025, with stricter rules for 'systemic-risk' models.
terms.applies_to: Providers of general-purpose AI models placed on the EU market

terms.definition: A change to a high-risk AI system after market placement that affects compliance with the AI Act or the system's intended purpose. Triggers a fresh conformity assessment.
terms.applies_to: Providers and deployers modifying high-risk AI systems

terms.definition: Cryptographic technique that combines a shared secret with a hash function (e.g. SHA-256) to prove a message was sent by a known party and not altered. Used to sign webhook payloads and attestations.
terms.applies_to: API integrations, webhook security, audit trails

terms.definition: NIST-approved 256-bit cryptographic hash function, widely used to fingerprint files, sign records and seed HMAC. Considered safe for current use.
terms.applies_to: Anyone hashing or signing data

terms.definition: Cryptographically signed statement that a piece of evidence (e.g. a tachograph download, a model output, a config) existed in a given state at a given time. Forms the building block of an audit trail.
terms.applies_to: AI providers, fleet operators, compliance teams

terms.definition: Sequence of attestations chained together via hashes so that any tampering is detectable. Used to evidence GDPR, AI Act and DVSA compliance over time.
terms.applies_to: Operators producing tamper-evident compliance evidence

terms.definition: Time-ordered, tamper-evident record of significant events (user actions, system decisions, data changes) used by auditors and regulators to reconstruct what happened. Required by GDPR, AI Act and FCA rules.
terms.applies_to: Anyone subject to compliance obligations

terms.definition: Verifiable record of where data, models or content came from and how they were processed. Increasingly expected for AI training data and for media (e.g. C2PA Content Credentials).
terms.regulator: n/a (industry standards e.g. C2PA, W3C PROV)
terms.applies_to: AI developers, content publishers, regulated industries

terms.full_name: Good Distribution Practice for Medicinal Products
terms.definition: EU/UK rules requiring controlled storage, transport and traceability of human medicines, including temperature mapping and excursion handling. Audited by MHRA in the UK.
terms.applies_to: Pharma wholesalers, brokers, transport providers

terms.definition: UK licence required by anyone wholesale-distributing human medicines, including some 3PL and transport operators. Covers GDP compliance and a Responsible Person.
terms.applies_to: UK wholesalers and brokers of human medicines

terms.definition: Any deviation outside the approved storage/transport temperature range for sensitive product (often 2–8°C for vaccines, –20°C for biologics). Must be logged, risk-assessed and signed off by the QP/RP.
terms.applies_to: Pharma cold-chain operators and customers

terms.definition: Named individual under UK/EU pharma law who is personally accountable for batch release (QP) or GDP compliance (RP). Must be on every WDA(H) and MIA.
terms.applies_to: Pharma manufacturers, wholesalers, brokers

terms.full_name: Regulation (EC) No 1/2005 on the Protection of Animals During Transport
terms.definition: EU/UK rules requiring authorised transporters, trained drivers, fit vehicles and journey logs when moving live vertebrates for commercial purposes. Enforced via APHA inspections.
terms.regulator: APHA / Defra (UK); EU member-state competent authorities
terms.applies_to: Livestock hauliers and assembly centres

terms.definition: Three risk-based UAS categories: Open (low risk, no auth), Specific (operational authorisation with SORA risk assessment), Certified (manned-grade certification for high-risk ops). Used by CAA and EASA.
terms.applies_to: Drone operators and remote pilots

terms.definition: UK successor to EU ETS covering power, aviation and energy-intensive industry, with road transport not yet included. Sets the carbon-price backdrop for fleet electrification.
terms.applies_to: UK power, industrial and aviation operators (road excluded)

terms.full_name: Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment Directive
terms.definition: EU/UK rules requiring producers and carriers of waste electronics to register, label, separate and report on collection and recycling. Relevant for reverse-logistics fleets.
terms.applies_to: Producers, retailers and waste carriers of EEE

terms.definition: EU rules requiring around 50,000 firms (and large non-EU groups with EU turnover) to report ESG performance under ESRS standards from FY2024 onwards. Scope and timing being reviewed under Digital Omnibus.
terms.applies_to: Large EU undertakings, listed SMEs and large non-EU groups

terms.definition: Global trade body representing road transport associations and operator of the TIR guarantee chain. Issues TIR Carnets via national associations.
terms.applies_to: International road transport operators
